prompt: Does this statement survive the evidence?
shortExplanation: Contemporaneous video showed Clinton arriving in Bosnia without sniper fire.
fullTruth: She later said she had misspoken about the trip.
context: The statement was used to emphasize foreign-policy experience.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the quoted statement and the evidence identified for this case.
lesson: When memory supports a speaker's status, check the original footage.

sources.note: Background used to evaluate the quoted statement.

claim: This was the largest audience ever to witness an inauguration, period.
prompt: Does this statement survive the evidence?
shortExplanation: Photographs, transit data, and television ratings did not support the claim.
fullTruth: The statement combined in-person and broadcast audiences without producing evidence for an all-time record.
context: It was delivered at the administration's first press briefing.
transcript: This was the largest audience ever to witness an inauguration, period.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the quoted statement and the evidence identified for this case.
lesson: Watch for a superlative claim whose measurement changes mid-argument.

sources.note: The spoken statement used in this round.

sources.title: Case background and record
sources.note: Background used to evaluate the quoted statement.

claim: I believe nicotine is not addictive.
prompt: Does this statement survive the evidence?
shortExplanation: Nicotine dependence was already well documented, and internal industry records showed extensive knowledge of its reinforcing effects.
fullTruth: Seven tobacco executives delivered closely aligned answers under oath.
context: The wording framed a factual assertion as a personal belief.
transcript: I believe nicotine is not addictive.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the quoted statement and the evidence identified for this case.
lesson: Turning a factual claim into 'I believe' does not make contrary evidence disappear.

sources.title: Case background and record
sources.note: Background used to evaluate the quoted statement.

claim: I had nothing to do with Laci's disappearance.
prompt: Does this statement survive the evidence?
shortExplanation: Peterson was convicted of murdering Laci Peterson and their unborn son.
fullTruth: The prosecution presented evidence about his movements, his affair, his statements, and the location where the bodies were recovered.
context: He gave media interviews while the search was underway.
transcript: I had nothing to do with Laci's disappearance.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the quoted statement and the evidence identified for this case.
lesson: Separate a person's denial from the independent evidence placing events in context.

sources.title: Source video
sources.note: The spoken statement used in this round.

sources.title: Case background and record
sources.note: Background used to evaluate the quoted statement.

claim: I did not stage this attack.
prompt: Does this statement survive the evidence?
shortExplanation: A jury found that Smollett made false reports about the alleged attack.
fullTruth: His conviction was later overturned because prosecutors had previously promised not to prosecute him, not because a court found the account true.
context: This verdict is limited to the statement and the trial record, while noting the later procedural ruling.
transcript: I did not stage this attack.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies only to the quoted statement and the evidence identified for this case.
lesson: A procedural reversal is not necessarily a factual exoneration. Read what the ruling actually decided.
